cataloging_subject.label: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The exact 1904 Paues edited selection represented by the pinned repository XML: title matter, twenty-three main prose components, four Appendix I prose versions, and a zero-item Appendix II variants apparatus. It is neither one medieval work nor one complete manuscript, codex, Bible, or New Testament; Paues assembled it from five witnesses, and no single witness supplies every layer.

catalog_summary.title: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator(s) and compiler
catalog_summary.date: Fourteenth century, probably with distinct Southern and Midland layers; first printed privately 1902; revised publication 1904; electronic encoding 2006
catalog_summary.region: England; composite Southern or South-Western and Midland or North-East Midland textual layers

identifiers.scheme: Library of Congress call number in source header

titles.value: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version

agents.name: Anna Carolina Paues
notes: Editor of the private 1902 thesis printing and revised 1904 Cambridge edition.

agents.name: University of Michigan Library
notes: Publisher/provider of the 2006 DLPS electronic encoding and linked page images.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator(s)
notes: The number and identity of translators are unresolved; Paues distinguishes a Southern nucleus from Midland additions.

agents.name: Anonymous medieval compiler
notes: The edited selection reflects a composite vernacular collection addressed to a religious woman; no named compiler is established.

agents.name: Miss Haggitt
notes: Paues records borrowing her transcript of Bodleian MS Douce 250; the full identity, transcript history, and present location are unresolved.

scope.description: Middle English translation responsibility across the twenty-seven count-bearing prose components; this does not assert one translator

notes: Paues’s dialectal and textual analysis indicates at least a Southern nucleus and Midland additions; assigning one translator would overstate the evidence.

scope.description: The medieval collection represented by the main Prologue, Epistles, Acts, and Matthew

notes: This is a functional cataloging responsibility for the composite collection, not an identification of a person or a claim that all components originated together.

scope.description: The 1904 source edition, including selection, correction, headings, collation, Appendix I, and Appendix II
The 1902 private thesis predecessor containing the Prologue and main biblical text but not the 1904 appendices
At least the Douce 250 passages used or collated by Paues, mediated through the loaned transcript

notes: The exact extent and survival of Haggitt’s transcript remain unknown.

scope.description: 2006 OCR and Level-1 TEI-in-Libraries encoding with linked page images

date_statements.display: Fourteenth century; probably multiple translation layers
notes: Paues’s title and analysis support a fourteenth-century tradition, but later scholarship may refine whether every component is entirely fourteenth-century.

date_statements.display: Fourteenth century, perhaps the second half

scope.description: Assembly of the composite main collection, not composition of every translated component

date_statements.display: Latter fourteenth century in Cambridge University Library MS Dd.12.39
Privately printed in May 1902 as Paues’s Upsala doctoral thesis
Revised and publicly issued by Cambridge University Press in 1904, with separate introduction and two appendices

scope.description: Broad cultural origin; not a single demonstrated place of composition
Paues’s more specific South-Western characterization, with a possible Kent or South-East translator background
Midland additions to the collection, with Southern admixture
Paues’s probable North or North-East Midland source layer
Scribal dialect of Selwyn College MS 108.L.1’s Prologue and Epistles

notes: This describes the Selwyn scribe’s language, not the underlying translation’s place of composition.

scope.description: Modern editorial title, headings, apparatus, and omitted publication matter

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Vulgate comparison readings, biblical rubrics, headings, and brief apparatus phrases

notes: Latin is scoped as quoted or rubric apparatus and is not a co-primary language of the twenty-seven Middle English prose components.

scope.description: Primary form of the cataloged edited selection, determined by its twenty-seven count-bearing prose components; title, omitted wrappers, and Appendix II remain separately typed as editorial or structural matter

form_statements.description: All twenty-seven count-bearing textual components are prose.
Title, omitted wrappers, and variants apparatus are structural or editorial layers rather than additional prose works.

genre_statements.label: Scholarly edition
notes: This classifies Paues’s 1904 editorial presentation, not the anonymous medieval collection as originally a scholarly edition.

subject_statements.label: Religious women as intended readers
notes: The Prologue addresses a ‘sister’; this is audience evidence, not authorship or institutional provenance.

summaries.value: An edited Middle English scriptural collection comprising a doctrinal Prologue, the Catholic and Pauline Epistles except Philemon, Acts, and Matthew 1:1–6:13, supplemented by four alternative Catholic Epistle versions from the Douce tradition and a Holkham variants apparatus. Paues’s witnesses preserve different portions and textual states; the main text principally follows Selwyn College MS 108.L.1, with Acts from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.12.39.

The cataloged subject is the exact Paues/U-M edited selection, not all matter in the 1904 printed volume, not the 1902 thesis as a whole, and not a blanket identity with any one medieval witness. Twenty-seven non-overlapping prose components are counted once; Appendix II is apparatus with zero additional items.

Paues distinguishes a Southern or South-Western nucleus (Prologue, Peter, James, 1 John, and the Pauline Epistles) from Midland additions, especially 2–3 John, Jude, Acts, and Matthew; the exact number, order, and geography of translation and compilation events remain unresolved.

The pinned XML contains 12,162 elements: 6 DIV1, 27 DIV2, 134 DIV3, 31 HEAD, 202 P, 350 PB, 3,843 MILESTONE, 3,018 NOTE1, 351 EPB, 3 TRAILER, and 4,150 HI elements. The main text has 23 DIV2 components, Appendix I has four, and Appendix II has none.

Middle English is primary for all twenty-seven count-bearing prose components. Modern English supplies editorial framing and apparatus; Latin occurs only in quoted Vulgate readings, rubrics, headings, and brief apparatus phrases.

extent.description: Twenty-seven non-overlapping prose items: twenty-three main-text components and four Appendix I alternative versions. Thirty substantive sections comprise the main container plus 23 leaves, Appendix I container plus 4 leaves, and Appendix II apparatus. The full content_parts hierarchy has 33 nodes after adding the zero-count selection root, title wrapper, and consolidated omitted-matter wrapper.

content_parts.label: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version (1904 edited selection)
content_parts.summary: The exact edited selection represented by the repository XML: title matter, Paues’s main Prologue-and-New-Testament text, Appendix I’s four Douce-tradition versions, and Appendix II’s Holkham variants; excluded front and back matter is recorded structurally but not counted as a textual item.
notes: This is an edited selection assembled from five witnesses and editorial apparatus, not one medieval work or one complete New Testament.
The 350-image source package includes the whole photographed printed block, but the selected textual content occupies title images 2–4 and textual images 87–315.

content_parts.label: 1904 title matter
content_parts.summary: Transcribed title page naming the edition and Anna C. Paues.

content_parts.label: Omitted publication matter
content_parts.summary: Consolidated structural record for the photographed half-title, untranscribed introduction and other front matter, and untranscribed back matter.
notes: Non-contiguous omitted matter is one zero-count structural wrapper; it is image-linked and page-break-linked but not part of the transcribed 27-item textual selection.

content_parts.label: Main text
content_parts.summary: Paues’s main edited text: Prologue, Catholic and Pauline Epistles, Acts, and Matthew 1:1–6:13.
notes: The boundary page image 00000086 carries printed p. lxxxvi before the main-text start and is not assigned to a count-bearing leaf.

content_parts.label: Appendix II: variants from Holkham Hall MS 672
content_parts.summary: Zero-item editorial apparatus recording Holkham witness variants against the already printed main text.
notes: The apparatus has 22 P and 4 PB elements but no DIV2, DIV3, MILESTONE, or NOTE1 elements, so it does not add a twenty-eighth prose item.

related_works.title: Latin Vulgate New Testament
notes: The Middle English components translate Vulgate scripture, but no specific physical Latin exemplar for the medieval translator(s) is identified.

related_works.title: Codex Amiatinus Vulgate
notes: Paues used Codex Amiatinus as an editorial comparator for Vulgate readings; it is not asserted as the medieval translator’s physical exemplar.

related_works.title: Wycliffite Middle English Bible
notes: The collection is cataloged as an independent non-Wycliffite translation tradition; comparison does not assert derivation.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME XML for ajg4507.
evidence.locator: HEADER, TITLEPAGE, FRONT, BODY, BACK, and all descendant elements
evidence.summary: Supplies the exact repository artifact, title, source statement, electronic editorial declaration, declared language, page-image links, text, apparatus, and structural boundaries.

evidence.citation: Element, hierarchy, page-sequence, section, part, and note-allocation audit of the pinned ajg4507 XML.
evidence.locator: All 12,162 elements; DIV1/DIV2 allocation and page sequence 00000001–00000350
evidence.summary: Verifies 6 DIV1, 27 DIV2, 134 DIV3, 31 HEAD, 202 P, 350 PB, 3,843 MILESTONE, 3,018 NOTE1, 351 EPB, 3 TRAILER, and 4,150 HI elements; supports the count-once model of 27 prose items, 30 substantive sections, and 33 hierarchy nodes.

evidence.citation: Language and apparatus audit of the pinned ajg4507 XML.
evidence.locator: 54 NOTE1 instances explicitly naming Vulg. or Vulgate; Latin headings and Appendix II abbreviations
evidence.summary: Confirms Middle English as the primary text while locating quoted Latin Vulgate readings and Latin rubrics or headings; supports quoted rather than co-primary Latin.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for Paues’s 1902 and 1904 editions and their complete scans.
evidence.summary: Supports the title, editor, private 1902 predecessor, 1904 revision, textual boundary, five-witness source account, editorial practices, dialect analysis, intended audience, and appendices.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for the five witnesses and Haggitt transcript intermediary.
evidence.summary: Supports witness sigla S, P, C, D, and H; source scopes; the absence of one full-selection witness; and mediation of Douce 250 readings through Haggitt’s loaned transcript.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for Cambridge, Selwyn College MS 108.L.1.
evidence.summary: Supports the current shelfmark, early-fifteenth-century date, contents and folios, parchment extent, Herefordshire scribal dialect, and one-scribe modern catalog description.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 434.
evidence.summary: Supports the current shelfmark, title, contents, public complete facsimile, image license, and Paues’s classification of P as a copy of S used for collation.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for Cambridge University Library MS Dd.12.39.
evidence.summary: Supports the parchment manuscript’s latter-fourteenth-century date, 74-leaf extent, independent non-Wycliffite Acts at ff. 16–72, and Paues’s use of it as the Acts base witness.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for Bodleian MS Douce 250 and the Haggitt transcript.
evidence.summary: Supports the current manuscript description, date, support, extent, ARKs, and the distinct mediated edition chain through Haggitt’s transcript.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for Bodleian MS Holkham misc. 40, formerly Holkham Hall MS 672.
evidence.summary: Supports the current and historical shelfmarks, first-quarter-fifteenth-century date, Epistles at ff. 133r–162va, physical description, and Appendix II’s variant scope.

evidence.citation: ajg4507 lineage evidence for the raw U-M package and repository normalization comparison.
evidence.summary: Supports exact raw package contents, image sequence and quality, embedded XML hash, semantic equivalence with the repository XML, and the qualified copied_from relation.

open_questions.question: How many translation and compilation events produced the Southern nucleus and Midland additions, and what exact dates, places, and Latin exemplars underlie each component?
next_steps: Collate all five witnesses and relevant collateral biblical manuscripts against the Vulgate tradition.
Consult recent scholarship on Selwyn College MS 108.L.1 and non-Wycliffite Middle English scripture.
Keep dialectal, scribal, compositional, and exemplar evidence distinct.

open_questions.question: Who was Miss Haggitt, what portion of Douce 250 did her transcript cover, did Paues also inspect the manuscript directly, and where does the transcript now survive?
next_steps: Search Paues correspondence, Upsala thesis papers, Cambridge University Press archives, and Bodleian reader or reproduction records.
Do not collapse the transcript intermediary into direct edition-to-manuscript transcription without evidence.

open_questions.question: How should Paues’s claim of a second hand at Selwyn ff. 86r–101v be reconciled with Ker and the current MWM catalog’s one-scribe description?
next_steps: Inspect Selwyn MS 108.L.1 directly or through new high-resolution images.
Compare the relevant folios palaeographically and consult Ker 1977 and recent Selwyn scholarship.

open_questions.question: Are complete current public facsimiles or orderable reproductions available for Selwyn 108.L.1, CUL Dd.12.39, Douce 250, or Holkham misc. 40?
next_steps: Contact the four holding institutions and recheck their current digital-library catalogs.
Record exact image coverage and terms before adding any facsimile relation.

open_questions.question: What transfer and transformation history connects the May 2006 raw U-M package, the current public U-M presentation, and the normalized repository XML?
next_steps: Seek U-M DLPS ingest and migration documentation and compare a current downloadable serialization if one becomes available.
Retain copied_from only for the byte-compared raw package and keep the raw-to-current relation qualified as version_of.

open_questions.question: How do Paues’s historical Holkham Hall 672 locators and siglum H map exactly onto the current MS Holkham misc. 40 foliation, especially the current catalog endpoint ‘162va’?
next_steps: Inspect the manuscript or a complete reproduction and reconcile old and modern foliations.
Preserve the current TEI locator literally until the endpoint can be verified.

notes: Count axes are deliberately separate: 27 numbered prose items, 30 substantive sections, and 33 explicit content-part hierarchy nodes.
Witness dates, physical descriptions, holdings, image access, and image rights remain in the lineage manifest and do not become undifferentiated whole-selection properties.

No single witness carries the complete cataloged selection: S and P cover the main collection, C supplies the Acts base, D underlies Appendix I through a transcript, and H supplies Appendix II variants.
Latin is quoted or rubric language only; the primary textual language of every count-bearing component is Middle English.
